content:
# Luke Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 110-111 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_018_page_001.jpg, LUK_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Then Jesus told them a parable about their need to pray at all times and never give up. **2** He said, "In a certain city there was a judge who had no fear of God and no respect for people. **3** In that same city there was a widow who kept coming to him and saying, 'Grant me justice against my adversary.' **4** For a long time he refused, but eventually he said to himself, 'Even though I do not fear God or respect anyone, **5** yet because this widow keeps wearing me out, I will grant her justice — otherwise she will keep coming and exhaust me completely.'"

**6** Then the Lord said, "Listen to what the unjust judge says. **7** Will not God bring about justice for his chosen ones who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep putting them off? **8** I tell you, he will see that they get justice, and quickly. But when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?"

**9** He also told this parable to certain people who were confident in their own righteousness and looked down on everyone else: **10** "Two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. **11** The Pharisee stood by himself and prayed like this: 'God, I thank you that I am not like the rest of people — greedy, dishonest, adulterous — or even like this tax collector. **12** I fast twice a week; I give a tenth of everything I earn.' **13** But the tax collector stood at a distance and would not even lift his eyes toward heaven. Instead, he beat his chest and said, 'God, have mercy on me, a sinner.' **14** I tell you, this man went home justified before God rather than the other. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who humbles himself will be exalted."

**15** People were also bringing their infants to Jesus so that he might touch them. When the disciples saw this, they rebuked them. **16** But Jesus called for the children and said, "Let the little children come to me, and do not try to stop them, for the kingdom of God belongs to people like these. **17** I tell you the truth: anyone who does not receive the kingdom of God like a child will never enter it."

**18** A certain ruler asked him, "Good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life?" **19** Jesus said to him, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. **20** You know the commandments: 'Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not give false testimony, honor your father and mother.'" **21** The man said, "I have kept all of these since I was young." **22** When Jesus heard this, he said to him, "There is still one thing you lack. Sell everything you own and distribute the money to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come and follow me." **23** When the man heard this, he became deeply grieved, because he was extremely wealthy.

**24** Jesus looked at him and said, "How hard it is for those who have wealth to enter the kingdom of God! **25** In fact, it is easier for a camel to squeeze through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God." **26** Those who heard this said, "Then who can possibly be saved?" **27** Jesus replied, "What is impossible for human beings is possible for God."

**28** Peter said, "Look, we have left everything we had and followed you." **29** Jesus said to them, "I tell you the truth: there is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God **30** who will not receive many times more in this present age, and in the age to come, eternal life."

**31** Then he took the twelve aside and said to them, "Listen, we are going up to Jerusalem, and everything that has been written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled. **32** He will be handed over to the Gentiles. He will be mocked, treated with contempt, and spit upon. **33** They will flog him and kill him, and on the third day he will rise again." **34** But the twelve understood none of this. The meaning of these words was hidden from them, and they did not grasp what he was saying.

**35** As Jesus was approaching Jericho, a blind man was sitting beside the road, begging. **36** When he heard a crowd passing by, he asked what was happening. **37** They told him, "Jesus of Nazareth is passing by." **38** So he shouted, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!" **39** Those at the front of the crowd told him sharply to be quiet, but he shouted all the louder, "Son of David, have mercy on me!"

**40** Jesus stopped and ordered the man to be brought to him. When he came near, Jesus asked him, **41** "What do you want me to do for you?" He said, "Lord, I want to see again." **42** Jesus said to him, "Receive your sight. Your faith has healed you." **43** Instantly the man could see, and he began following Jesus, praising God. And all the people, when they saw it, gave praise to God.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

- **18:1-5 — The persistent widow and the unjust judge**: The parable opens with Luke's rare editorial comment explaining its purpose: PROS TO DEIN PANTOTE PROSEUCHESTHAI AUTOUS KAI MĒ ENKAKEIN (pros to dein pantote proseuchesthai autous kai mē enkakein, "that they must always pray and not lose heart"). The verb ENKAKEIN means "to grow weary," "to become discouraged," or "to give up" — it is the exhaustion that comes when prayers seem to go unanswered. The judge is described with two negatives: TON THEON MĒ PHOBOUMENOS (ton theon mē phoboumenos, "not fearing God") and ANTHRŌPON MĒ ENTREPOMENOS (anthrōpon mē entrepomenos, "not respecting people"). He is doubly immune to moral pressure — neither divine accountability nor social shame can move him. The widow's weapon is sheer persistence. Her repeated demand — EKDIKĒSON ME APO TOU ANTIDKOU MOU (ekdikēson me apo tou antidikou mou, "vindicate me against my adversary") — uses the verb EKDIKEŌ, which means "to grant justice" or "to avenge." The judge's eventual capitulation is comically self-interested: he acts not from conscience but because the widow HYPOPIAZĒ ME (hypopiazē me, "is giving me a black eye" or "is wearing me out"). The verb HYPOPIAZŌ literally means "to strike under the eye" — a boxing term. The parable works by contrast, not comparison: if even a corrupt judge yields to persistent pleading, how much more will a righteous God respond to the cries of his people?

- **18:7-8 — "Will he find faith on the earth?"**: God's response to his EKLEKTŌN (eklektōn, "chosen ones" or "elect") who cry out HĒMERAS KAI NYKTOS (hēmeras kai nyktos, "day and night") will come EN TACHEI (en tachei, "quickly" or "speedily"). This phrase does not necessarily mean "immediately" but "without unnecessary delay" — when God acts, he acts decisively. The closing question — ARA HO HUIOS TOU ANTHRŌPOU ELTHŌN HEURĒSEI TĒN PISTIN EPI TĒS GĒS (ara ho huios tou anthrōpou elthōn heurēsei tēn pistin epi tēs gēs, "when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?") — is devastating in its rhetorical force. The definite article TĒN before PISTIN (the faith) suggests not faith in general but the specific faith of persistent prayer — the widow-like tenacity that keeps crying out when the answer has not yet come. The question hangs unanswered, addressed directly to the reader across the centuries.

- **18:9-14 — The Pharisee and the tax collector**: The parable's audience is precisely identified: people who PEPOITHOTAS EPH' HEAUTOIS HOTI EISIN DIKAIOI (pepoithotas eph' heautois hoti eisin dikaioi, "trusted in themselves that they were righteous") and EXOUTHENOUNTAS TOUS LOIPOUS (exouthenountas tous loipous, "despised the rest"). The Pharisee's prayer is a masterpiece of spiritual self-deception. He STATHEIS PROS HEAUTON (statheis pros heauton, "stood by himself" or "stood praying to himself") — the ambiguity is telling: was he standing apart from others, or was his prayer directed not to God but to his own ego? His thanksgiving is actually a comparison: he lists sins he does not commit (HARPAGES, ADIKOI, MOICHOI — "greedy, unjust, adulterers") and then points to the tax collector as a living exhibit of the sin he has avoided. His fasting twice a week exceeded the requirement (the Law prescribed only one annual fast, on the Day of Atonement), and his tithing of PANTA HOSA KTŌMAI ("everything I acquire") went beyond the tithing laws. He is, by every measurable standard, a righteous man. That is the parable's sting. The tax collector, by contrast, stands MAKROTHEN (makrothen, "far off"), will not even lift his eyes, and beats his STĒTHOS (stēthos, "chest") — a gesture of grief and contrition. His prayer is five words in Greek: HO THEOS HILASTHĒTI MOI TŌ HAMARTŌLŌ (ho theos hilasthēti moi tō hamartōlō, "God, be merciful to me, the sinner"). The verb HILASTHĒTI is related to HILASTĒRION, the mercy seat — the place where sacrificial blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement. He is not merely asking for kindness; he is asking for atonement. Jesus' verdict — this man went home DEDIKAIŌMENOS (dedikaiōmenos, "justified" or "declared righteous") — uses the same root (DIKAIOS) that the Pharisee claimed for himself. The one who declared himself righteous was not; the one who declared himself a sinner was.

- **18:15-17 — Children and the kingdom**: Luke uses the word BREPHĒ (brephē, "infants" or "babies") rather than the PAIDIA ("children") found in the parallel accounts — these are the very youngest, perhaps still being carried. The disciples' rebuke reflects the cultural assumption that infants have nothing to contribute to a rabbi's ministry. Jesus' response reverses the assumption entirely: TŌN GAR TOIOUTŌN ESTIN HĒ BASILEIA TOU THEOU (tōn gar toioutōn estin hē basileia tou theou, "for to such as these belongs the kingdom of God"). The "childlikeness" Jesus requires is not innocence or naivety but the posture of complete dependence — a baby has nothing to offer, nothing to bargain with, and no status to leverage. This connects directly to the preceding parable: the tax collector entered the kingdom because he came with empty hands, like a child. The Pharisee was shut out because he came with a resume.

- **18:18-23 — The rich ruler**: The questioner is identified as an ARCHŌN (archōn, "ruler" or "official") — a person of social authority. His address DIDASKALE AGATHE (didaskale agathe, "good teacher") prompts Jesus' startling counter-question: TI ME LEGEIS AGATHON? OUDEIS AGATHOS EI MĒ HEIS HO THEOS (ti me legeis agathon? oudeis agathos ei mē heis ho theos, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone"). Jesus is not denying his own goodness but forcing the ruler to reckon with the implications of his own words. If only God is truly good, then calling Jesus "good" either means something profound about who Jesus is — or it means nothing at all. The commandments Jesus lists are all from the "second table" — duties toward other people (Exodus 20:12-16), not duties toward God. The ruler's claim to have kept them all APO NEOTĒTOS (apo neotētos, "from my youth") is not challenged by Jesus. Instead, Jesus identifies the one thing that remains: PANTA HOSA ECHEIS PŌLĒSON KAI DIADOS PTŌCHOIS (panta hosa echeis pōlēson kai diados ptōchois, "sell everything you have and distribute to the poor"). The ruler's response — PERILUPOS EGENĒTHĒ (perilupos egenēthē, "he became deeply grieved") — reveals that his wealth was not merely something he possessed but something that possessed him. He had kept every commandment about loving his neighbor, except the one that would cost him everything.

- **18:24-27 — The camel and the needle's eye**: Jesus' image of a KAMĒLON (kamēlon, "camel") passing through a TRUPĒMATOS BELONĒS (trupēmatos belonēs, "eye of a needle") is deliberately absurd — the largest animal commonly seen in Palestine paired with the smallest opening in daily life. Various interpreters have tried to soften the image by suggesting a small gate called "the Needle's Eye" or a mistranslation of KAMILON ("rope") for KAMĒLON ("camel"), but these are later inventions. Jesus means exactly what he says: it is impossible. The crowd's alarmed response — KAI TIS DYNATAI SŌTHĒNAI (kai tis dynatai sōthēnai, "then who can be saved?") — shows they understood wealth as a sign of divine blessing. If the blessed cannot be saved, who can? Jesus' answer — TA ADYNATA PARA ANTHRŌPOIS DYNATA PARA TŌ THEŌ ESTIN (ta adynata para anthrōpois dynata para tō theō estin, "what is impossible with humans is possible with God") — does not soften the impossibility. It relocates it. Salvation is not difficult; it is impossible — on human terms. It requires divine intervention. This is the theology of the tax collector's prayer compressed into a single sentence.

- **18:28-30 — The rewards of discipleship**: Peter's declaration — HĒMEIS APHENTES TA IDIA ĒKOLOUTHĒSAMEN SOI (hēmeis aphentes ta idia ēkolouthēsamen soi, "we have left our own things and followed you") — is both a genuine statement of sacrifice and, perhaps, a veiled question: what will we receive? Jesus' reply lists what has been abandoned: OIKIAN (oikian, "house"), GYNAIKA (gynaika, "wife"), ADELPHOUS (adelphous, "brothers"), GONEIS (goneis, "parents"), TEKNA (tekna, "children"). The Sinaiticus text includes "wife" in this list, which is unique among the Synoptic parallels. The promise is twofold: POLLAPLASIONA (pollaplasiona, "many times more") in this present age — referring to the new family of the community of believers — and in the coming age, ZŌĒN AIŌNION (zōēn aiōnion, "eternal life"). Jesus does not promise that discipleship will be costless, but that the returns will be disproportionate to the investment — both now and forever.

- **18:31-34 — The third passion prediction**: This is the most detailed of Jesus' three predictions of his death in Luke, and it explicitly ties the coming suffering to prophetic fulfillment: TELESTHĒSETAI PANTA TA GEGRAMMENA DIA TŌN PROPHĒTŌN TŌ HUIŌ TOU ANTHRŌPOU (telesthēsetai panta ta gegrammena dia tōn prophētōn tō huiō tou anthrōpou, "everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled"). The passive verbs pile up: PARADOTHĒSETAI (paradothēsetai, "he will be handed over"), EMPAICHTHĒSETAI (empaichthēsetai, "he will be mocked"), HYBRISTHĒSETAI (hybristhēsetai, "he will be treated with contempt"), EMPTYSTHĒSETAI (emptysthēsetai, "he will be spit upon"), MASTIGŌSANTES (mastigōsantes, "having flogged"), APOKTENOUSIN (apoktenousin, "they will kill"). The subject of these verbs is TOIS ETHNESIN (tois ethnesin, "the Gentiles" or "the nations") — the crucifixion will be a Roman execution, not a Jewish one. The prediction ends with resurrection: TĒ HĒMERA TĒ TRITĒ ANASTĒSETAI (tē hēmera tē tritē anastēsetai, "on the third day he will rise"). Luke then adds an extraordinary editorial comment in verse 34: the disciples understood OUDEN TOUTŌN (ouden toutōn, "none of these things"). The word was KEKRYMMENON (kekrymmenon, "hidden" or "concealed") from them — the passive voice suggests divine concealment, not merely human dullness. They could hear the words but could not grasp the meaning. The cross was, at this point, literally incomprehensible to those closest to Jesus.

- **18:35-43 — The blind man near Jericho**: Luke places this healing as Jesus approaches Jericho (ENGIZEIN, engizein, "drawing near"), while Mark (10:46) places it as Jesus leaves Jericho — a well-known discrepancy among the Synoptic Gospels. The blind man's cry — IĒSOU HUIE DAUID ELEĒSON ME (Iēsou huie Dauid eleēson me, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!") — is a messianic confession. "Son of David" is a royal title with political implications; the blind man is publicly declaring Jesus to be the long-awaited king of Israel. The crowd's attempt to silence him — EPETIMŌN AUTŌ HINA SIGĒSĒ (epetimōn autō hina sigēsē, "they rebuked him so that he would be quiet") — reflects either embarrassment at the messianic title or annoyance at a beggar's disruption. But like the widow before the judge, the blind man refuses to be silenced: POLLŌ MALLON EKRAZEN (pollō mallon ekrazen, "he cried out all the more"). Jesus' question — TI SOI THELEIS POIĒSŌ (ti soi theleis poiēsō, "what do you want me to do for you?") — is striking because the answer is obvious. But Jesus insists on hearing the request articulated, just as the widow had to keep stating her case. The man's answer — KYRIE HINA ANABLEPSŌ (kyrie hina anablepsō, "Lord, that I may see again") — and Jesus' response — HĒ PISTIS SOU SESŌKEN SE (hē pistis sou sesōken se, "your faith has saved you") — echo exactly the words spoken to the Samaritan leper in 17:19. The verb ANABLEPŌ means both "to look up" and "to recover sight" — the physical and spiritual are intertwined. The chapter that began with a parable about persistent prayer ends with a living example of it: a man who would not stop crying out until he was heard.

- **18:1-43 — The chapter as a whole**: Luke 18 is organized around a single question: who can enter the kingdom of God? The answer, given through a series of surprising reversals, is consistently the same: those who come with nothing. The persistent widow comes with nothing but her plea. The tax collector comes with nothing but his sin. The infants come with nothing at all. The rich ruler, who comes with everything, goes away empty. The blind beggar comes with nothing but his voice — and receives everything. Each scene strips away another layer of human self-sufficiency: moral achievement (the Pharisee), social status (the ruler), even physical capacity (the blind man). What remains when everything is stripped away is the raw cry for mercy — and that cry, Jesus teaches, is the only prayer God never refuses. The chapter's two bookend parables — the widow and the blind man — share the same structure: a powerless person cries out to a powerful one, is initially ignored or opposed, refuses to stop, and ultimately receives what was asked. This is Luke's portrait of saving faith: not theological sophistication, not moral perfection, but relentless, undignified, desperate persistence directed at the right person.
